title: Living in Bouder vlog 1 -  15  - 2017: Showing my Denarius from Antoninus Pius

enrichment.summary: Kyle vlogs from Boulder, sharing updates on his Minecraft/3D modeling project recreating ancient Rome, particularly the Colosseum. He then shows off a Roman denarius coin featuring Antoninus Pius that his mom gave him for his birthday, reflecting on its historical significance and his personal memories of visiting Rome.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: His model will be superior because you can actually enter and navigate it, unlike Google's SketchUp version
Finds it remarkable and meaningful to own a piece of actual Roman history that still looks like a modern coin
Deeply impressed that Roman culture and language (Latin) connects directly to the present day

transcript.text: Hello living in Boulder vlog. It's only 6.45 p.m. on Monday, so I'm like fresh. I'm super high though. Um, I don't think I did a video for a few days. Uh, last time I did the video, uh, I think I was working on the rope. Um, around, the ropes around the Coliseum to make it look like a giant tent.
Um, yeah, I finished. I did. Um, and then, um, so now I'm working on the Coliseum and I want to make the Coliseum look, uh, as realistic as possible. You know, I want to see if I can outdo Google.

Google has a boo-boos or they didn't finish. They didn't make it completely. It looks really good from a distance, like way better than what I could do is they use SketchUp program, but they don't, you can't go inside it. You know, there's this, uh, you could enter the Coliseum from both the North and Southern ends on ground level.

And it just went right through into the Coliseum. And so if you want to do a demo, you know, like if I wanted to do a screen capture of a video of me riding my bike into the Coliseum in ancient Rome, um, you'd want to use me because then it just blocks, you know, there's no through.

So, um, yeah, but, uh, and then, so I was working on the seats yesterday and, uh, they go up to like 49 meters or something, but yeah, it's almost where you can do every meter because you can do it in increments of a meter on, uh, the, uh, polygon tool, but I can almost do that.

So some of them have to be the same level, but it's, you know, it's pretty close. So it's pretty cool. It's pretty steep. Um, oh, and yesterday I was definitely, uh, made sure to look on ground level all around the Coliseum. That was really cool.

I was there in 97 for three days and I was walking around the Coliseum for like, you know, an hour or whatever. And I remember it still in my head. I remember feeling like it was a modern day Coliseum. I was very impressed with that. Just picturing myself in the past 2000 years ago inside of, I was inside of the stairwell, the original stairwell going up, um, to the colony on the second floor.

And on the side, there were these, uh, trenches for the water. So, and, oh, and then I was in the colony looking down. I'm like, oh my God, this is, this is just like a modern day Coliseum. Only it was narrower. Everything was narrower, but it's almost as big as a modern day Coliseum.
Um, yeah, so, uh, and then, so today I got up, oh, and I was working. What was I working on yesterday? It made me, oh, there's those bleachers, I think.
Like, and just researching and stuff. So I was working until like seven. And, um, yeah, and so by the time I went to sleep, it was, you know, it was about time.
So then I got up late and I didn't have much daylight, but, um, so I haven't really done much work today.
But today the goal was to, uh, uh, rebuild the area around the arena. Um, and so I was researching what the emperor's box looks like.
And so there is this, on Platinum and Ashby, it didn't describe all that stuff. The Platinum and Ashby book is this book. Uh, it's on my website.
It's like the Bible of all of the, uh, the buildings. It was made, written a hundred years ago, but it has like 1800 different buildings.
But so I typed in seating in the Roman Coliseum and there was this article and they were, they were describing all these words for pieces of furniture that I'd never heard of.
And, um, and, um, because, uh, in the Coliseum there's the, um, there's the podium that all of the senators sit on.
It goes around and it's flat and they have their chairs on it. It's 15 feet wide.
And, um, but the emperor's on something that's even higher and it has a special name, like the polvium or something.
And that's on these, these, these, these, these, these, these columns that have another name that I can't even remember.
And then he has a bed that has another name that I can't remember.
But the thing, the polvium thing, I typed that polvium Rome just to get a picture of it.
You know, to get a picture in my head what I want to make this, his, uh, emperor area look like.
And, um, and third row down, first column on the left there is this coin that I just recognize.
So let me, let me flash back.
So yeah, my mom, my mom, uh, for my birthday, like a couple of weeks ago, summer 27th, I turned 44.
